original_abstract:
Abstract

Mineral surfaces play a complex role in regulating the activity of extracellular enzymes in soils and sediments, typically providing a protective effect that extends their functional lifespan despite initial activity suppression through adsorption. This study investigates how the oxygenation of reduced iron-bearing minerals—a common process in redox-oscillating environments—alters this dynamic by generating reactive oxygen species (ROS). Using the cellulose-degrading enzyme β-glucosidase (BG) and a suite of reduced minerals (nontronite, montmorillonite, and magnetite), the research demonstrates that while BG adsorption under anoxic conditions increases enzyme longevity, the introduction of oxygen triggers the production of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and other ROS. The yield of these oxidants, specifically •OH, was found to be positively correlated with the extent of structural Fe(II) oxidation. Crucially, these ROS induced conformational changes and structural decomposition of the BG molecule, leading to significant inactivation and a shortened functional lifespan. These findings reveal that in oxic, iron-rich environments, the inhibitory impact of ROS outweighs the traditional protective benefits of mineral adsorption. This direct mechanism of enzyme inactivation provides essential insights into the stability of the active enzyme pool and the broader regulation of carbon cycling in fluctuating redox environments.
humanized_abstract:
Abstract

Mineral surfaces can govern the activity of extracellular enzymes in soils and sediments, and have generally been reported to provide a protective role, which leads to a prolonged activity (i.e., longer activity lifetime). This study used the model enzyme β-glucosidase (BG) and reduced iron-bearing minerals (nontronite, montmorillonite and magnetite) to investigate how the widespread process of oxygenation of reduced iron-bearing minerals which leads to the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) affects enzyme adsorption and activity. Our results showed that although enzyme adsorption under anoxic conditions enhanced enzyme stability, addition of oxygen under the anoxic adsorbed BG resulted in the production of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and other ROS. The amount of •OH produced was closely related to the degree of structural Fe(II) oxidation in the minerals. The ROS induced structural change and degradation of the BG enzyme, resulting in severe enzyme inactivation and decreased enzyme functional lifetime shortening. Our results showed that in iron-rich and oxygen-rich environments, the effect of ROS on enzyme inactivation can counteract the enzyme adsorption and surface protection effect, thus directly affecting the stability of active enzyme pool and thus impacting carbon cycle in redox-oscillating environments.
